faults.name: Piler Auto Lost
faults.description: Piler has lost its automatic positioning reference. The system cannot confirm current location within the racking structure. Often caused by encoder or homing sensor issues.
probable_causes: Encoder failure or encoder cable damaged — check connections at motor and PLC module
Homing/reference sensor blocked or misaligned — LED not solid when at home position
PLC position register corrupted after power loss or brown-out
Drive fault caused mid-travel stop — piler stopped between positions
Mechanical obstruction preventing full travel to home position
Final-down proximity sensor alignment too marginal — target plate barely entering sensing zone
fix_steps: Check encoder cable connections at motor and PLC module — reseat both ends
Inspect homing/reference sensor — clean lens, verify alignment, LED must be solid ON at home
Manually jog piler to home position and re-reference via HMI
Check PLC position register values (D-register or MW area) — compare to expected home value
Verify drive is healthy — check for overcurrent or overvoltage faults on VFD display
If encoder replaced, recalibrate travel distances in PLC program
Lower lift completely — hold DOWN button extra 3-5 seconds past normal stop
Turn inhibit key OFF then back ON to clear condition
Hit Alarm Silence / Reset on HMI
faults.field_trick: Manually press/hold the bottom limit sensor target while watching fault. If fault clears = sensor alignment issue. If not = electrical/PLC input issue.

faults.name: Piler Fork Overtravel
faults.description: Fork extension has exceeded its maximum allowed travel distance, triggering a safety stop.
probable_causes: Fork extend limit switch failed or wiring broken
Hydraulic valve sticking — fork continues extending after command removed
PLC output stuck ON due to relay welding
Limit switch actuator bent or misaligned
Encoder on fork drive giving incorrect feedback
fix_steps: Verify fork extend limit switch operation manually — actuate by hand, watch PLC input LED
Check hydraulic directional valve — use manual override to test
Inspect PLC output module — check for welded relay contacts with multimeter
Realign or replace limit switch actuator bracket
Test fork drive encoder separately to confirm counts match actual travel

faults.name: Piler Traverse Position Error
faults.description: Piler horizontal traverse position does not match expected location. Cannot confirm alignment with target bay.
probable_causes: Traverse encoder wheel slipping on rail — worn rubber tire on encoder wheel
Bar code or reflector positioning system misread — dirty reader lens
Rail obstruction causing traverse motor to stall
Traverse drive belt or chain skip — teeth worn or tension lost
PLC traverse position counter accumulated error over multiple cycles
fix_steps: Check traverse encoder wheel contact with rail — replace if rubber tire is worn smooth
Clean bar code reader or reflector lens — use isopropyl alcohol and lint-free cloth
Walk the rail — check for debris, fallen product, or hardware on travel path
Inspect traverse drive belt/chain tension and tooth engagement
Re-home piler traverse by driving to reference mark and resetting position counter
If using bar code positioning, verify all bay labels are clean and not peeling

faults.name: Conveyor Jam Detected
faults.description: Photo-eye or motor current sensing indicates product jam on conveyor section. Conveyor stopped to prevent damage.
probable_causes: Product misaligned or fallen on conveyor — physical blockage
Accumulation zone full — downstream equipment stopped or faulted
Belt or chain slipping — worn sprocket or low tension
Photo-eye blocked by debris, dust, or condensation on lens
VFD tripped on overcurrent protecting motor from overload
fix_steps: Clear jammed product manually — check for damage to product and conveyor
Verify downstream equipment is running and accepting product
Inspect belt/chain tension and sprocket wear — adjust or replace as needed
Clean photo-eyes and reflectors with soft cloth, verify alignment with test mode
Check VFD display for fault code — reset if overcurrent was transient
Monitor motor current after clearing — sustained high amps indicates mechanical binding

faults.name: Conveyor Belt Tracking Fault
faults.description: Belt has migrated off-center, triggering tracking sensor or causing edge damage.
probable_causes: Uneven loading causing belt drift to one side
Tail pulley misalignment — one side tighter than other
Belt splice failing or belt stretched unevenly
Tracking roller jammed or seized bearing
Frame not level — foundation settling or structural shift
fix_steps: Stop conveyor immediately — inspect belt edge for fraying or damage
Adjust tail pulley snub bolts to re-track belt — quarter turn at a time
Check belt splice integrity — replace if separating or delaminating
Free or replace seized tracking rollers — spin each by hand to check
Verify frame level with precision level — shim if needed
Run conveyor at slow speed and observe tracking — make incremental adjustments

faults.name: Conveyor Motor Overtemperature
faults.description: Motor thermal protection tripped. Motor is too hot to operate safely.
probable_causes: Overloaded conveyor — too much product weight
Motor cooling fan failed or air intake blocked by debris
Ambient temperature too high — poor ventilation in area
Motor bearing failing — increased friction generating heat
VFD running motor below nameplate speed without external cooling
fix_steps: Let motor cool before restarting — minimum 15 minutes
Check motor fan — spin by hand, verify it turns with motor
Clear any debris blocking motor air intake and cooling fins
Feel motor bearings (carefully) — excessive heat or noise indicates failure
Check conveyor loading — reduce product if overloaded
If motor runs at reduced speed via VFD, add external cooling fan
Check motor current draw vs nameplate — sustained over FLA indicates problem

faults.name: Proximity Sensor Fault — No Target Detected
faults.description: Proximity sensor expected to detect a target is not triggering. Equipment has stopped waiting for confirmation signal.
probable_causes: Sensor face contaminated — dust, grease, metal shavings blocking detection
Target moved out of sensing range — mechanical drift or bracket loosened
Sensor cable damaged or connector loose — intermittent or no signal
Sensor failed internally — no output regardless of target presence
Wrong sensor type installed — inductive sensor for non-metal target or vice versa
fix_steps: Clean sensor face with non-abrasive cloth — remove dust, grease, metal particles
Verify sensing gap — adjust bracket to bring target within rated sensing distance
Check cable from sensor to input module — wiggle test while monitoring input LED
Substitute with known-good sensor of same type to confirm failure
Verify sensor type matches target material — inductive for metal, capacitive for non-metal
Check PLC input status directly — sensor LED ON does not always mean clean PLC input

faults.name: Proximity Sensor Fault — Stuck ON
faults.description: Proximity sensor output is continuously active regardless of target presence. Causes false position confirmations.
probable_causes: Sensor shorted internally — output transistor failed closed
Wiring short between signal wire and +V supply
Metal debris accumulated on inductive sensor face
PLC input module channel failed high — reads ON regardless of sensor
Electrical noise from nearby VFD cables inducing false signal
fix_steps: Disconnect sensor from wiring — if PLC input remains ON, it is a wiring or module issue
Clean sensor face, remove any metal debris or buildup
Check cable routing — separate sensor cables from VFD and motor power cables by 6 inches minimum
Swap sensor signal to adjacent PLC input channel to rule out module failure
Replace sensor if internal short confirmed — match part number exactly

faults.name: Photoelectric Sensor Fault
faults.description: Photoelectric sensor (through-beam, retro-reflective, or diffuse) not functioning correctly. Product detection unreliable.
probable_causes: Lens dirty — dust, moisture, or product residue on emitter or receiver
Reflector damaged or misaligned (retro-reflective type)
Emitter or receiver beam angle shifted — mounting vibration
Ambient light interference — sunlight or high-bay LED strobing
Sensitivity set incorrectly — too high causes false triggers, too low misses product
fix_steps: Clean both emitter and receiver lenses with soft lint-free cloth
For retro-reflective type: clean reflector, verify it is square to sensor beam
Check mounting brackets — tighten all hardware, verify beam alignment
If ambient light issue, add sensor hood or switch to modulated frequency type
Adjust sensitivity using teach button or potentiometer — follow sensor manual
Verify wiring: NPN vs PNP output must match PLC input type

faults.name: Safety Circuit Fault — E-Stop Active
faults.description: Emergency stop circuit is open. All motion disabled until circuit is restored and acknowledged at HMI.
probable_causes: E-stop button physically pressed — intentional or accidental contact
E-stop wiring break in series circuit — corroded terminal or broken conductor
Safety relay module fault — monitoring circuit detecting wiring issue
Light curtain triggered and latched — object in beam path
Gate interlock switch open — access door not fully closed and latched
fix_steps: Walk the entire machine — check every E-stop button, twist/pull to reset each one
Check safety relay module LED indicators for fault channel identification
Verify all gate interlock switches — close all access gates fully until latch clicks
Check light curtain alignment — clear any obstructions from beam path
If all devices are clear, check wiring continuity through entire E-stop loop with multimeter
Reset safety relay module and acknowledge fault at HMI panel

faults.name: Safety Circuit Fault — Guard Door Open
faults.description: Machine guard door interlock is open. Motion inhibited in the affected zone until door is closed and circuit resets.
probable_causes: Guard door physically open — maintenance access or not fully latched
Interlock switch actuator misaligned after door adjustment or replacement
Interlock switch failed — no output when door is fully closed
Wiring to interlock switch damaged — cut by door hinge movement
Safety controller not detecting interlock input — module issue
fix_steps: Close guard door fully — verify latch engagement, push until it clicks
Check interlock switch actuator alignment — tongue must fully insert into switch body
Test interlock switch with multimeter for continuity when door is closed
Inspect wiring from switch to safety controller — look for wear near hinges
Reset safety controller and acknowledge fault at HMI

faults.name: Safety Circuit Fault — Light Curtain
faults.description: Safety light curtain beam is interrupted or has alignment fault. Zone motion stopped.
probable_causes: Object or person in light curtain beam path
Light curtain lenses dirty — reduced beam strength causes fault
Emitter and receiver misaligned — vibration shifted mounting
Ambient light interference — direct sunlight hitting receiver
Light curtain internal fault — LED segment failed
fix_steps: Clear beam path — remove any objects, verify no one is in the zone
Clean light curtain lenses on both emitter and receiver with soft cloth
Check alignment — most light curtains have alignment LEDs, all should be green
If sunlight issue, add shielding or relocate to avoid direct sun exposure
Check light curtain diagnostic display for specific segment fault
Reset safety system and acknowledge at HMI after beam path is confirmed clear

faults.name: VFD Fault — Overcurrent
faults.description: Variable frequency drive tripped on overcurrent protection. Motor stopped immediately.
probable_causes: Mechanical overload — jammed product, seized bearing, or obstruction
Motor winding shorted — phase-to-phase or phase-to-ground insulation failure
VFD output IGBT degraded — outputting distorted waveform
Acceleration time too short for load inertia — current spike on start
Loose motor cable connection causing arcing and current spikes
fix_steps: Check motor and driven equipment for mechanical binding — try to rotate by hand
Megger motor windings — insulation resistance should be greater than 1 Megohm to ground
Inspect motor cable connections at VFD output terminals and motor terminal box — tighten all
Review VFD fault log for pattern — single event vs recurring at same point in cycle
Increase acceleration ramp time if fault occurs during motor start
If motor and wiring test good, VFD output section may need factory service

faults.name: VFD Fault — Overvoltage
faults.description: VFD DC bus voltage exceeded safe threshold, typically during deceleration when motor regenerates energy back to drive.
probable_causes: Deceleration time too short — regenerative voltage spike exceeds DC bus rating
Dynamic braking resistor open circuit or undersized for application
Incoming power voltage too high — check utility supply
Load has high inertia and is overdriving the motor during stop
Braking transistor in VFD failed — cannot dissipate regen energy
fix_steps: Increase deceleration ramp time in VFD parameters — try doubling current setting
Check dynamic braking resistor — measure resistance with multimeter, verify wiring
Measure incoming AC voltage at VFD input terminals — should be within drive rating
Enable coast-to-stop mode for high-inertia loads if application allows it
Check VFD braking transistor output — consult drive manual for test procedure

faults.name: VFD Fault — Ground Fault
faults.description: VFD detected current leakage to ground on motor output circuit. Drive tripped to protect equipment.
probable_causes: Motor cable insulation damage — pinched, abraded, or cut by sharp edge
Moisture in motor terminal box — condensation or water ingress
Motor winding insulation breakdown — age or overheating damage
Cable gland not sealed — water entering conduit and reaching connections
VFD ground fault detection threshold too sensitive for long cable runs
fix_steps: Disconnect motor leads at VFD — megger each phase to ground, all should read over 1 Megohm
Inspect motor cable entire run for physical damage — pay attention to conduit entry points
Open motor terminal box — check for moisture, corrosion, or water pooling
If cable and motor test good, adjust VFD ground fault sensitivity threshold per manual
Seal all cable glands and conduit entry points with appropriate sealant

faults.name: VFD Fault — Communication Loss
faults.description: VFD lost communication with PLC over fieldbus network. Drive stopped or went to fault safe state.
probable_causes: Network cable disconnected or damaged between PLC and VFD
VFD network module crashed — needs power cycle
PLC communication module overloaded — too many nodes or scan rate too fast
Network switch or hub failure in the path between PLC and VFD
VFD station/node address changed or duplicated on network
fix_steps: Check network cable at VFD communication port — reseat connector
Verify network switch or hub between PLC and VFD — check power and link LEDs
Power cycle the VFD to reset communication module
Verify VFD node/station address matches PLC configuration — check for duplicate addresses
If using Profinet/EtherNet-IP, ping VFD IP address from laptop to confirm network path
Check PLC communication module status for error counters or diagnostic codes

faults.name: PLC Communication Fault — Network Timeout
faults.description: PLC lost communication with a remote I/O rack, HMI, or networked device. Affected stations show comm fault.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable disconnected or damaged at either end
Network switch port failed or switch lost power
Remote I/O module crashed — needs power cycle to recover
IP address conflict on the industrial network — duplicate assignment
PLC network module firmware issue after recent update
fix_steps: Check physical Ethernet connections at both ends — reseat RJ45 connectors
Verify network switch status — check power LED and port link/activity LEDs
Ping remote device from a laptop on the same network segment
Power cycle the remote I/O rack or faulted device
Check for IP address conflicts — review DHCP leases or static IP assignment table
If firmware was recently updated, verify compatibility between PLC and network module versions

faults.name: PLC Communication Fault — Serial Link Down
faults.description: Serial communication link (RS-232/RS-485) between PLC and device has failed. Data exchange stopped.
probable_causes: Serial cable disconnected or pins bent in DB9/DB25 connector
Baud rate or parity mismatch after parameter change on one end
RS-485 termination resistor missing or wrong value on bus end
Serial port on PLC or device has hardware failure
Electrical noise on serial line from nearby VFD or motor power cables
fix_steps: Reseat serial cable at both ends — inspect for bent or broken pins
Verify communication parameters match at both ends: baud rate, parity, stop bits, station address
Check RS-485 termination — 120 ohm resistor required at each physical end of the bus
Route serial cable away from power cables and VFD wiring — 6 inch minimum separation
Substitute serial cable with known-good cable to rule out cable failure
Test with serial monitor software on laptop to confirm data is being sent/received

faults.name: PLC Battery Low
faults.description: PLC backup battery voltage is low. Risk of losing program and data if power is removed.
probable_causes: Battery has reached end of life — typically 3-5 year replacement cycle
Battery not making good contact in holder — corrosion on terminals
Frequent or extended power outages draining battery faster than normal
Wrong battery type installed during last replacement
fix_steps: Replace battery with PLC powered ON — this preserves program during swap
Use correct battery type per PLC manual — do NOT substitute different chemistry
Clean battery holder contacts before inserting new battery
Verify program is backed up to memory card or laptop before battery swap as safety measure
Record battery replacement date on PLC enclosure door for tracking

faults.name: Palletizer Pattern Fault
faults.description: Palletizer cannot complete the current layer pattern. Product count or position mismatch detected.
probable_causes: Product size changed without updating pattern recipe in HMI
Infeed conveyor delivering product too fast — products double-stacking
Gripper or vacuum head not picking all products in row
Layer sheet dispenser jammed or empty
Product counting photo-eye miscounting — dirty or misaligned
fix_steps: Verify product dimensions match the active pattern recipe on HMI
Adjust infeed conveyor speed or accumulation zone timing to match palletizer cycle
Check gripper vacuum level — inspect vacuum cups for cracks, tears, or debris
Reload layer sheet magazine — clear any sheet jams in dispenser mechanism
Clean and verify product counting photo-eyes — test with single product pass

faults.name: Packaging Machine Seal Fault
faults.description: Heat seal bar temperature or pressure out of specification. Packages may have incomplete seals.
probable_causes: Seal bar heater element burned out — open circuit
Thermocouple failed — reading incorrect temperature to controller
Seal bar pressure cylinder low on air supply
Seal bar surface contaminated with melted film residue
Temperature controller needs auto-tune after heater element replacement
fix_steps: Check seal bar temperature with IR thermometer — compare to controller display reading
Inspect heater element — measure resistance with multimeter, compare to nameplate
Verify air supply pressure to seal cylinder — minimum 80 PSI required
Clean seal bar surfaces — remove film buildup carefully with brass brush or scraper
If heater was recently replaced, run auto-tune on temperature controller
Replace thermocouple if temperature reading is erratic or significantly different from IR reading

faults.name: Stretch Wrapper Fault — Film Break
faults.description: Stretch wrapper film has broken during wrap cycle. Pallet is partially wrapped.
probable_causes: Film tension set too high — exceeding film stretch capacity
Film catching on pallet corner or product edge — sharp protrusion
Film roll installed incorrectly — wrong direction of unwind
Film pre-stretch rollers worn or damaged — causing uneven stretch
Ambient temperature too cold — film becomes brittle and tears easily
fix_steps: Re-thread film through pre-stretch assembly and attach to pallet
Reduce film tension/force setting on wrapper controller — try 10% reduction
Inspect pallet load for sharp corners or protruding edges that could cut film
Verify film roll is installed with correct unwind direction per machine manual
Check pre-stretch rollers for wear, flat spots, or buildup — clean or replace
In cold environments, store film rolls in warm area before use

faults.name: AS/RS Crane Position Fault
faults.description: AS/RS crane cannot confirm its position in the aisle. Storage and retrieval operations halted.
probable_causes: Laser distance sensor dirty or misaligned — reading incorrect distance
Aisle end reference marker missing or damaged
Crane travel wheel slipping on rail — worn wheel or oily rail surface
Position encoder cable damaged by crane travel movement
PLC position tracking accumulated error over many cycles without re-homing
fix_steps: Clean laser distance sensor lens — use appropriate lens cleaning tissue
Verify aisle end reference marker is present and reflective surface is intact
Check crane travel wheels for wear — replace if wheel diameter is below minimum spec
Inspect encoder cable along entire travel path — look for pinch points or wear from repeated flexing
Initiate crane re-homing sequence from maintenance HMI screen
Clean rail surface if oily — use degreaser on travel path

faults.name: Servo Position Error
faults.description: Servo drive reports position following error exceeds tolerance. Axis stopped to prevent damage.
probable_causes: Mechanical binding or obstruction in axis travel path
Encoder feedback cable loose, damaged, or disconnected at motor
Servo gain parameters incorrect — too low causes sluggish response, too high causes oscillation
Motor brake not releasing — brake solenoid or power issue
Mechanical coupling between motor and load slipping — keyway or setscrew
fix_steps: Check for mechanical obstruction — disable drive, try to move axis by hand
Inspect encoder cable at motor connector and drive connector — reseat both ends
Listen for motor brake release click when drive enables — no click means brake issue
Verify coupling between motor shaft and load — check setscrew torque and keyway condition
Check servo drive fault display for specific sub-code — note it before resetting
Reset and home the axis before attempting normal operation

faults.name: Pneumatic Pressure Low
faults.description: Air pressure below minimum operating threshold. Pneumatic actuators may not function correctly or at all.
probable_causes: Compressor not running or not keeping up with system demand
Major air leak in supply line, fitting, cylinder, or valve
Pressure regulator failed or adjusted too low
Filter/regulator/lubricator assembly clogged — restricting airflow
Drain valve on receiver tank stuck open or leaking
fix_steps: Check main air pressure gauge at FRL unit — note current reading
Verify compressor is running — check motor contactor and pressure switch
Listen for air leaks — use soapy water spray on suspect fittings, hoses, and cylinders
Check pressure regulator setting — adjust to spec, typically 80-100 PSI for most equipment
Inspect and replace air filter element if dirty or clogged
Check condensate drain — verify auto-drain is functioning or manually drain receiver tank

faults.name: Motor Starter Overload Trip
faults.description: Motor starter thermal or electronic overload relay has tripped, cutting power to the motor to prevent winding damage from sustained overcurrent.
probable_causes: Motor mechanically overloaded — jammed product, seized bearing, or obstruction on driven equipment
Overload relay heater size incorrect for motor FLA — too small causes nuisance trips
Single-phasing condition — one supply phase lost causing remaining phases to draw excess current
Ambient temperature around starter enclosure too high — relay trips at lower current in hot panel
Motor winding partially shorted — drawing above nameplate current under normal load
fix_steps: Let overload cool 5 minutes, then press reset button on the overload relay
Measure motor current on all three phases with clamp meter — compare to nameplate FLA
Check for single-phasing: measure voltage across all three phases at starter — should be balanced within 2%
Verify overload heater or electronic overload setting matches motor nameplate FLA
Check driven equipment for mechanical binding — try to rotate by hand with motor disconnected
If ambient temperature is high, consider upsizing overload or improving panel ventilation
Megger motor windings if overcurrent persists with no mechanical cause
faults.field_trick: If overload trips repeatedly within minutes of reset, do NOT keep resetting — there is a real overcurrent issue. Measure current before resetting again.

faults.name: Encoder Feedback Loss
faults.description: Servo or motor drive has lost encoder feedback signal. Axis position is unknown and motion has stopped.
probable_causes: Encoder cable disconnected at motor or drive end — connector not fully seated
Encoder cable damaged — pinched, cut, or flex fatigue from repeated motion
Encoder power supply failed — 5V or 24V supply to encoder not present
Encoder itself failed — internal electronics or disc damaged
Electrical noise from nearby VFD cables coupling into encoder signal wires
fix_steps: Check encoder cable connections at both motor and drive — reseat connectors firmly
Measure encoder supply voltage at the motor connector — should be 5V or 24V depending on type
Inspect encoder cable along its entire run for damage, pinch points, or tight bend radius
If cable has been repeatedly flexed, replace with high-flex rated encoder cable
Swap encoder with known-good unit if cable and power check out
Verify encoder cable is routed at least 6 inches away from VFD output power cables
faults.field_trick: With the drive disabled, slowly rotate the motor shaft by hand while monitoring the encoder count on the drive display. If the count does not change smoothly, the encoder or cable is faulty.

faults.name: Hydraulic Pressure Low
faults.description: Hydraulic system pressure is below the minimum operating threshold. Actuators may move slowly, stall, or not move at all.
probable_causes: Hydraulic pump worn — internal bypass leaking, not building pressure
Hydraulic fluid level low — check reservoir sight glass
Relief valve set too low or stuck open — bypassing fluid back to tank
Major external leak at hose, fitting, or cylinder seal
Hydraulic filter clogged — restricting flow to pump inlet causing cavitation
Pump drive coupling broken or motor not running
fix_steps: Check hydraulic fluid level at reservoir sight glass — fill if low with correct fluid type
Inspect all hoses, fittings, and cylinders for visible leaks — look under machine for puddles
Check hydraulic pump motor — verify it is running and coupling is intact
Read system pressure at the gauge — compare to nameplate or setup sheet spec
Check and replace hydraulic filter if pressure drop indicator is showing clogged
If pressure still low with no leaks, relief valve may need adjustment or pump may need replacement
Check hydraulic fluid temperature — overheated fluid loses viscosity and reduces pressure

faults.name: Air Pressure Fault
faults.description: Plant air or local pneumatic supply pressure has dropped below the machine's minimum operating requirement. Pneumatic actuators may not function.
probable_causes: Plant air compressor down or not keeping up with demand
Air line to machine disconnected, kinked, or valve shut off
Large air leak on machine — blown hose, cracked fitting, or failed cylinder seal
Pressure regulator at machine FRL unit failed or set too low
Pressure switch on machine incorrectly calibrated or failed
fix_steps: Check main plant air pressure gauge — if low, the issue is upstream of the machine
Verify the supply valve to the machine is fully open
Check FRL (filter-regulator-lubricator) unit — read pressure gauge, adjust regulator if needed
Listen for air leaks — spray soapy water on suspect fittings, hoses, and cylinder end caps
Verify pressure switch setpoint matches machine requirements — typically 75-90 PSI minimum
Check air filter element in FRL — replace if dirty or clogged
Drain water from FRL bowl and any low-point drains in the air piping
faults.field_trick: Close the main supply valve, watch the machine pressure gauge. If it drops quickly, there is a leak on the machine. If it holds, the supply is intermittent.

faults.name: Temperature Sensor Fault — Thermocouple Break
faults.description: Temperature controller or PLC reports thermocouple open circuit (upscale burnout). Temperature reading is invalid — may show maximum range or error code.
probable_causes: Thermocouple wire broken — fatigue from heat cycling or mechanical damage
Thermocouple terminal connection loose at controller or junction box
Thermocouple sheath damaged — element exposed to process and corroded through
Wrong thermocouple type connected — Type J in Type K input gives wrong reading then error
Extension wire splice failed — corrosion or loose crimp at junction
fix_steps: Check thermocouple connections at the controller terminal strip — tighten screws
Measure resistance across thermocouple leads at controller — open circuit confirms break
Inspect thermocouple probe at process insertion point — look for sheath damage or corrosion
Verify thermocouple type matches controller configuration — J, K, T, etc.
If thermocouple is broken, replace with correct type and length
Check extension wire connections at any intermediate junction boxes — re-crimp or replace
After replacement, verify reading against a known reference or second temperature instrument
faults.field_trick: Short the thermocouple input terminals together at the controller. The reading should go to ambient (room) temperature. If it does, the controller input is working — the problem is the thermocouple or wiring.

faults.name: Communication Timeout — EtherNet/IP
faults.description: EtherNet/IP communication between PLC and remote device has timed out. CIP connection lost or I/O data exchange stopped.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable disconnected, damaged, or wrong cable type (crossover vs straight-through)
Network switch failure or port failure on switch
Remote device crashed or locked up — needs power cycle
IP address conflict — two devices with same IP on the network
RPI (Requested Packet Interval) set too aggressive for network conditions
PLC scanner module overloaded with too many connections
fix_steps: Check Ethernet cable connections at both PLC and remote device — reseat connectors
Verify network switch port LEDs — link and activity should both be lit
Ping the remote device IP address from a laptop on the same subnet
Power cycle the faulted remote device — wait 30 seconds for full restart
Check for duplicate IP addresses — review IP assignment spreadsheet or scan the network
If using managed switch, check for VLAN or port security configuration blocking traffic
Increase RPI timeout if connection drops intermittently under network load

faults.name: Communication Timeout — DeviceNet
faults.description: DeviceNet node has stopped communicating. Scanner reports node offline and associated I/O data is invalid.
probable_causes: DeviceNet cable trunk or drop cable disconnected or damaged
Node power supply failed — DeviceNet requires 24VDC on the bus
Termination resistor missing from end of DeviceNet trunk — must have 121 ohm at each end
Node MAC address changed or conflicts with another node
DeviceNet scanner module needs parameter re-download or power cycle
fix_steps: Check DeviceNet cable connections at the faulted node — push connectors fully in
Measure 24VDC at the faulted node DeviceNet port — should be between 11V and 25V
Verify 121 ohm termination resistors are installed at both physical ends of the trunk
Check node MAC address DIP switches — compare to scanner configuration
Power cycle the faulted node and watch for it to come back online in scanner diagnostics
If node does not return, try replacing with spare and setting same MAC address

faults.name: Communication Timeout — Profinet
faults.description: Profinet IO device has stopped responding. PLC IO controller reports device offline and outputs go to safe state.
probable_causes: Profinet cable disconnected or RJ45 connector damaged
IO device has lost power or experienced internal fault
Device name not assigned or was accidentally cleared
Network infrastructure issue — switch port down or cable too long (100m max)
Firmware version mismatch between GSD file and actual device firmware
fix_steps: Check Profinet cable at the faulted device — reseat RJ45 connector, check for damage
Verify device has power — check power LED on the Profinet IO device
From engineering station, check if device name is correctly assigned — re-assign if blank
Check managed switch diagnostics for the port connected to the faulted device
Verify cable length is under 100 meters — use cable tester to confirm
If device was recently replaced, ensure GSD file matches and device name is set correctly

faults.name: Servo Drive Following Error
faults.description: Servo axis position error has exceeded the allowable following error window. The commanded position and actual position differ by more than the configured tolerance.
probable_causes: Mechanical obstruction or binding preventing axis from reaching commanded position
Servo motor brake not releasing — motor cannot turn freely
Servo gain parameters need tuning — too low causes lag, too high causes oscillation
Encoder feedback error — cable noise, loose connection, or encoder fault
Load inertia changed — new tooling or product weight exceeds original servo sizing
Coupling between motor and load slipped — lost mechanical connection
fix_steps: Check for mechanical obstruction — disable drive, try to move axis by hand
Listen for motor brake release when drive enables — confirm brake releases cleanly
Check motor-to-load coupling — verify setscrew torque, keyway condition, or belt tension
Inspect encoder cable connections at motor and drive — reseat both ends
Review servo drive fault log for pattern — same position every time suggests mechanical issue
If load changed, servo gains may need re-tuning — contact controls engineer
Reset fault, re-home axis, and attempt motion at reduced speed to isolate issue
faults.field_trick: Run the axis at 10% speed. If it completes without faulting, the issue is dynamic — gains, inertia, or friction. If it still faults, the issue is static — obstruction, brake, or coupling.

faults.name: Robot Teach Pendant E-Stop
faults.description: Robot teach pendant emergency stop is activated. All robot motion stopped, servo power disabled.
probable_causes: Teach pendant E-stop button physically pressed — intentional or accidental
Teach pendant cable damaged — broken wire in E-stop circuit
Teach pendant connector not fully seated at robot controller
Teach pendant deadman switch released during teach mode
Robot controller detecting teach pendant communication fault
fix_steps: Check teach pendant E-stop button — twist clockwise to release it
Verify teach pendant cable is firmly connected to robot controller — reseat connector
Inspect teach pendant cable for damage — look for kinks, cuts, or crushed sections
Reset robot fault from teach pendant or controller panel after E-stop is released
If using deadman switch in teach mode, verify proper 3-position grip
If E-stop resets but immediately re-faults, teach pendant cable or internal switch may need replacement

faults.name: Barcode Scanner No-Read
faults.description: Barcode scanner failed to read or decode the barcode on the product. Product may be diverted or line stopped depending on configuration.
probable_causes: Barcode label damaged, wrinkled, smeared, or missing
Scanner lens dirty — dust, moisture, or product residue on optics
Product not positioned correctly — barcode not in scanner read window
Barcode print quality degraded — low contrast between bars and spaces
Scanner focus or gain settings incorrect for label distance or size
Ambient light interference — direct sunlight or high-bay LED flickering
fix_steps: Check the product that caused the no-read — inspect barcode label quality
Clean scanner lens with appropriate lens cleaning tissue or compressed air
Verify product orientation — barcode must face the scanner within its read angle
Test scanner with a known-good barcode label at the correct distance
Adjust scanner focus and gain using scanner configuration software if available
If intermittent, check for conveyor speed variation causing product to pass through read zone too fast
Verify barcode symbology configured in scanner matches actual barcode type on product

faults.name: Weight Scale Out of Tolerance
faults.description: In-line checkweigher or weight scale reading is outside the acceptable tolerance band. Product may be over or under the target weight.
probable_causes: Scale not zeroed — tare weight has drifted from buildup on conveyor or platter
Load cell damaged or overloaded — dropped heavy object on scale
Vibration from nearby equipment coupling into scale — causes noisy readings
Scale conveyor belt tension changed — dragging on frame adds false weight
Product recipe or target weight changed without updating scale parameters
Ambient temperature shift — large temperature changes affect load cell accuracy
fix_steps: Run zero calibration with scale empty — clear any debris from weigh platter or belt
Run span calibration with certified test weight — verify reading matches test weight
Check for vibration sources near the scale — isolate with vibration dampening mounts if needed
Inspect scale conveyor belt — verify it moves freely without dragging on the frame
Verify target weight and tolerance band settings match current product specification
Check load cell cable connections — corrosion or moisture in junction box causes drift
If readings are erratic, swap load cell with known-good unit to confirm failure

faults.name: Wrapper Film Break
faults.description: Film on stretch wrapper, flow wrapper, or shrink wrapper has broken during operation. Wrapping cycle interrupted.
probable_causes: Film tension set too high for the film gauge being used
Sharp corner on product or pallet load catching and tearing the film
Film pre-stretch ratio too aggressive — exceeding film's stretch capacity
Film roll quality issue — defective roll with thin spots or contamination
Pre-stretch rollers worn, nicked, or contaminated causing uneven stretch
Cold ambient temperature making film brittle and prone to tearing
fix_steps: Re-thread film through the pre-stretch assembly and reattach to product/pallet
Reduce film force/tension setting by 10-15% and test
Inspect product or pallet load for sharp edges that could cut film — add corner protectors
Check pre-stretch rollers for nicks, flat spots, or film residue buildup — clean or replace
Verify correct film gauge is loaded — thinner film breaks more easily
In cold environments, pre-condition film rolls by storing in heated area before use
If using stretch wrapper, reduce pre-stretch ratio and increase number of wraps to compensate

faults.name: Case Erector Jam
faults.description: Case erector has jammed — case blank stuck in the forming section, flap folding area, or bottom seal station. Machine stopped.
probable_causes: Case blank magazine loaded incorrectly — blanks upside down or backward
Case blank size does not match machine recipe — wrong blank loaded
Vacuum cups on case pickup not gripping — worn cups, low vacuum, or clogged filter
Flap folding plow bent or misaligned — not folding flaps cleanly
Hot melt glue not adhering — temperature too low or glue nozzle clogged
Pneumatic cylinder not fully extending — air pressure low or cylinder seal leaking
fix_steps: Clear the jammed case blank — remove all debris from forming area
Verify case blanks are loaded correctly in magazine — follow directional arrows on machine
Check vacuum cups on case pickup arm — replace cracked or worn cups
Verify vacuum level — check vacuum gauge, inspect filter for clogging
Inspect flap folding plows for damage or misalignment — adjust per setup sheet
Check hot melt glue temperature and nozzle condition — purge nozzle if clogged
Verify case blank size matches the recipe selected on HMI — changeover if needed

faults.name: Label Printer Fault
faults.description: Label printer/applicator has faulted. Labels may not be printing, applying incorrectly, or printer reports an error condition.
probable_causes: Label stock or ribbon out — media needs replacement
Printhead dirty or damaged — causing poor print quality or sensor errors
Label sensor not detecting label gap or black mark — sensor needs cleaning or adjustment
Applicator air cylinder not extending — air pressure issue or cylinder fault
Communication lost between printer and PLC or host system
Label stock winding incorrectly around printhead — causing jam
fix_steps: Check label stock and ribbon supply — reload if empty, verify correct media type
Clean printhead with approved printhead cleaning pen or IPA wipe — never use metal objects
Clean label gap sensor or black mark sensor with compressed air and IPA
Recalibrate label sensor — run the printer's media calibration routine
Check applicator air supply — verify pressure and cylinder operation
If communication error, check data cable between printer and PLC/host — reseat connections
Power cycle the printer after clearing any jams and reloading media

faults.name: Vision System Inspection Fail
faults.description: Machine vision system has flagged a product as failing inspection. Product may have print defect, wrong label, missing component, or dimensional error.
probable_causes: Actual product defect — print error, missing label, wrong color, dimensional issue
Camera lens dirty — contaminant on lens causing false rejects
Lighting changed — ambient light shift, bulb aging, or light fixture moved
Vision recipe does not match current product — wrong inspection program selected
Camera focus shifted — vibration or temperature change altered focal distance
Product positioning inconsistent — conveyor speed variation or guide rail gap
fix_steps: Check the rejected product — verify if the defect is real or a false reject
Clean camera lens with appropriate lens tissue and inspect lighting fixtures
Verify the correct vision recipe/program is selected for the current product run
Check lighting intensity and uniformity — replace aging bulbs or LEDs if dimmed
If false rejects are increasing, recalibrate or re-teach the vision inspection
Verify product positioning through the camera field of view — adjust guide rails if needed
Check camera focus — use live image display to verify sharpness on product features
Review reject history for patterns — same defect location may indicate upstream process issue
